quiz.title: Unit 4 Quiz 11, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Electron Geometry and Regions of Electron Density
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: VSEPR Theory, Electron Geometry, Regions of Electron Density, Lone Pairs in Electron Geometry, Molecular vs Electron Geometry

questions.question: What is the electron geometry of CH₄?
questions.explanation: CH₄ has four bonding pairs and no lone pairs around carbon, giving it a tetrahedral electron geometry with bond angles of 109.5°.

questions.question: Which element is NOT involved in hydrogen bonding?
questions.explanation: Chlorine is NOT involved in hydrogen bonding. Hydrogen bonding occurs only when hydrogen is bonded to highly electronegative small atoms: nitrogen, oxygen, or fluorine (N, O, F). These atoms have small sizes and high electronegativity, creating strong partial charges. Although chlorine is electronegative, its larger size makes the partial charge too diffuse for effective hydrogen bonding.

questions.question: Hydrogen bonding is strongest when hydrogen is bonded to:
questions.explanation: Hydrogen bonding is strongest when hydrogen is bonded to fluorine (F). Fluorine is the most electronegative element, creating the largest partial positive charge on hydrogen and the largest partial negative charge on itself. This results in the strongest electrostatic attraction between molecules. The strength of hydrogen bonding follows the trend: F–H···F > O–H···O > N–H···N. Carbon (D) does not participate in hydrogen bonding.

questions.question: Which type of bond must be present for hydrogen bonding to occur?
questions.explanation: A polar covalent bond between hydrogen and a highly electronegative atom (N, O, or F) must be present for hydrogen bonding to occur. This polar bond creates a partial positive charge on hydrogen and a partial negative charge on the electronegative atom, allowing electrostatic attraction between molecules. Hydrogen bonding is an intermolecular force, not an intramolecular bond, but it requires polar covalent bonds within molecules to create the necessary charge separation.

questions.question: Which compound shows hydrogen bonding?
questions.explanation: NH₃ (ammonia) shows hydrogen bonding because hydrogen is bonded to nitrogen (N), which is one of the three highly electronegative small atoms (N, O, F) that participate in hydrogen bonding. H₂S does not show significant hydrogen bonding because sulfur is too large and less electronegative. CH₄ has no hydrogen bonding (C is not electronegative enough), and CO₂ has polar bonds but no H bonded to N/O/F.

questions.question: Hydrogen bonding is a type of:
questions.explanation: Hydrogen bonding is a type of intermolecular force (attraction between molecules), not an intramolecular force (bonds within molecules). It occurs between a hydrogen atom covalently bonded to N, O, or F in one molecule and a lone pair on another electronegative atom in a neighboring molecule. Although stronger than other dipole-dipole forces, it is still much weaker than covalent or ionic bonds.

questions.question: The electron geometry of NH₃ is:
questions.explanation: NH₃ has three bonding pairs and one lone pair around nitrogen, giving it four regions of electron density. Therefore, its electron geometry is tetrahedral, even though its molecular shape is trigonal pyramidal.

questions.question: H₂O has how many electron regions around oxygen?
questions.explanation: Oxygen in H₂O has two bonding pairs and two lone pairs, giving it four regions of electron density. This makes its electron geometry tetrahedral, even though its molecular shape is bent.

questions.question: Which molecule has octahedral electron geometry?
questions.explanation: SF₆ (sulfur hexafluoride) has octahedral electron geometry. Sulfur has six bonding pairs and no lone pairs, giving it six regions of electron density arranged octahedrally with 90° bond angles.

questions.question: The electron geometry of CO₂ is:
questions.explanation: CO₂ has two double bonds (each counted as one region) and no lone pairs on carbon, giving it two regions of electron density. This results in a linear electron geometry with 180° bond angles.

questions.question: Which molecule has trigonal planar electron geometry?
questions.explanation: BF₃ has three bonding pairs and no lone pairs on boron, giving it three regions of electron density and trigonal planar electron geometry (120° bond angles).

questions.question: Electron geometry counts lone pairs as:
options: Twice a region of electron density
Half a region of electron density
questions.explanation: In VSEPR theory, each lone pair counts as one region of electron density, just like bonding pairs. Both lone pairs and bonding pairs contribute to the electron geometry.

questions.question: What is the electron geometry of PCl₃?
questions.explanation: PCl₃ has three bonding pairs and one lone pair on phosphorus, giving it four regions of electron density. Its electron geometry is tetrahedral, while its molecular shape is trigonal pyramidal.

questions.question: Which molecule has linear electron geometry?
questions.explanation: BeCl₂ has two bonding pairs and no lone pairs on beryllium, giving it two regions of electron density and a linear electron geometry (180°).

questions.question: The electron geometry of NO₃⁻ is:
questions.explanation: NO₃⁻ (nitrate ion) has three regions of electron density around nitrogen (resonance structures mean all bonds are equivalent) and no lone pairs, giving it trigonal planar electron geometry.

questions.question: The molecular shape of XeF₄ is:
questions.explanation: XeF₄ has 6 electron regions (octahedral electron geometry), but 2 are lone pairs. The 4 bonding pairs form a square planar molecular shape.

questions.question: The electron geometry of PCl₅ is:
questions.explanation: PCl₅ has five bonding pairs and no lone pairs on phosphorus, giving it five regions of electron density. This results in a trigonal bipyramidal electron geometry.

questions.question: XeF₄ has how many electron regions around Xe?
questions.explanation: XeF₄ has four bonding pairs and two lone pairs around xenon, giving it a total of six regions of electron density. This results in an octahedral electron geometry with a square planar molecular shape.

questions.question: When does molecular shape differ from electron geometry?
options: When there are only single bonds
When the central atom is carbon
When there are lone pairs on the central atom
questions.explanation: Molecular shape differs from electron geometry when lone pairs are present on the central atom. Lone pairs occupy space in the electron geometry but are invisible in the molecular shape.

questions.question: Electron geometry of SO₂ is:
questions.explanation: SO₂ has two bonding domains and one lone pair on sulfur, giving it three regions of electron density. Therefore, its electron geometry is trigonal planar, while its molecular shape is bent.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered electron geometry and VSEPR theory! 🧪📐
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review the difference between electron geometry and molecular shape.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on counting regions of electron density correctly.
scoreMessages.0: Please review VSEPR theory, electron regions, and how lone pairs affect geometry.
